title: Diffusion Counterfactual Generation with Semantic Abduction
abstract: Counterfactual image generation presents significant challenges, including preserving identity, maintaining perceptual quality, and ensuring faithfulness to an underlying causal model. While existing auto-encoding frameworks admit semantic latent spaces which can be manipulated for causal control, they struggle with scalability and fidelity. Advancements in diffusion models present opportunities for improving counterfactual image editing, having demonstrated state-of-the-art visual quality, human-aligned perception and representation learning capabilities. Here, we present a suite of diffusion-based causal mechanisms, introducing the notions of spatial, semantic and dynamic abduction. We propose a general framework that integrates semantic representations into diffusion models through the lens of Pearlian causality to edit images via a counterfactual reasoning process. To our knowledge, this is the first work to consider high-level semantic identity preservation for diffusion counterfactuals and to demonstrate how semantic control enables principled tradeoffs between faithful causal control and identity preservation.
impact: This paper demonstrates the trade-offs inherent in using diffusion models, widely optimised for L2-level random sampling, to perform L3-level counterfactual inference. By uncovering these trade-offs, our work demonstrates the need to evaluate diffusion models from a perspective that considers fairness and mitigates spurious correlations. Counterfactual inference shares strong similarities with text-driven image-to-image translation and editing, and our findings encourage practitioners to rethink the design of foundational diffusion models, which underpin these applications, moving beyond a focus on high-quality random sampling. Notably, in latent diffusion models, text control alone often fails to account for causal dependencies, instead defaulting to biases learned from training data, which can result in unintended consequences. In text-guided image editing, prompts alone cannot be used to infer causal structure, leaving the model to infer relationships based solely on correlations observed in training data. As a result, even minor edits can produce spurious changes. Adopting a structured causal perspective - where models implicitly discover or explicitly learn SCMs - enables developers to transparently state their model’s assumptions and update them as new information becomes available through the causal graph, and restricts misuse by enforcing causal rules. These considerations are critical in real-world applications like face modelling and medical imaging, where dataset biases can lead to flawed decision-making. In such high-stakes scenarios, ensuring that models are interpretable and transparent is essential, as black-box models often fail to provide the clarity needed to justify their outputs.

query_input.scenario: Natural image editing for professional content creation
query_input.issue: Causally inconsistent edits produce physically implausible scenes that degrade trust
hazard: Diffusion models manipulating high-level semantics without faithful causal mechanisms generate causally invalid but realistic counterfactuals.
exposure: Photographers and content creators relying on AI-controlled image edits

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Content creators lack methods to verify causal consistency in realistic AI edits, making decisions based on flawed outputs.
hipporag_result.impact: Trust in AI editing tools collapses, physically impossible scenes circulate, and professional reputations suffer.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Diffusion models generating causally invalid counterfactuals, through photographers and content creators relying on AI-controlled edits, trigger the inability to verify causal consistency, leading to collapse of trust, circulation of physically impossible scenes, and damaged professional reputations.

dose_response: Diffusion models manipulating semantics without causal mechanisms enable physically implausible edits, which when prioritized for speed by content creators lead to lost credibility, client distrust, and false imagery spread.
vulnerability: Content creators prioritize workflow speed over scrutinizing causal plausibility of AI edits
Lack of industry-wide standards for causal consistency in AI image generation tools
impact: Loss of professional credibility and client trust in AI-assisted content
Spread of plausible-but-false imagery distorting public perception of reality
